title: The Channel-Count Confound: A Continental Audit of Multi-Horizon Urban Growth Prediction
status: Accepted, sole author. PDF coming once SpatialDI posts it.
abstract: People keep reporting that ConvLSTMs beat plain CNNs at predicting urban growth further into the future. I checked that claim across 305M pixels of the continental US. Once you hold out 2020 properly, test cold on Lagos, and calibrate the uncertainty over 8.69M pixels, the ConvLSTM lead at longer horizons mostly goes away. It came from the number of input channels, not from the model learning anything better. The finding holds up under per-tile Wilcoxon tests and bootstrapping, and the calibration came in at r = 0.983.

title: Channel Count, Not Horizon Length, Drives Architectural Divergence in Multi-Horizon Urban Growth Prediction
status: Accepted, sole author. A six-page companion to the SpatialDI paper. PDF is on Zenodo.
abstract: The short companion to the SpatialDI paper. It makes the same point in six pages. When you push the forecast further out, you also end up feeding the model fewer input channels, so the two move together. What looks like a horizon effect in CNN versus ConvLSTM comparisons is mostly about that input design. Match the channels and the gap basically disappears.

title: Train Anywhere, Test Everywhere: Cross-Region Transfer in Earth Observation Is Decided by the Data, Not the Model
status: Still writing this one. Sole author, aimed at SIGSPATIAL 2026, and a follow-on to the channel-count work.
abstract: How well a model carries over to a new region turns out to be about the data, not the model. What matters is which region you test on and which input you feed in. It is not the architecture, not where you trained, and not the usual domain-generalization tricks. I trained one model per region and tested every model on every region, twenty regions in all, and two things came out of it. The first holds the input fixed. Then the test region sets the score and the training source barely counts. Training a model on its home region buys it almost nothing. The home-field edge is -0.001 FoM, which is basically noise. The regions rank the same way across six different architectures, fine-tuned foundation models included, and nothing on the model side helps. GroupDRO, pooling the sources, a 14x bigger model: none of them beat a one-line rule that just extends each region's recent past, which wins on FoM (0.56 against 0.34) and on a cleaner state metric (IoU 0.92 against 0.61). The second swaps the input and keeps the region fixed. Now transfer falls along a spectrum. Globally harmonized products carry over almost perfectly, raw sensor reflectance hardly carries at all (0.55), and a simple model-free probe predicts where a given input will land. The same pattern, the target mattering far more than the model, shows up again on the published PANGAEA benchmark. So the practical takeaway is a cheap check any cross-region benchmark can run before it trusts a gap. Built on GHSL, Landsat, Sentinel-1 and 2, MODIS NDVI and LST, ESA WorldCover, Copernicus DEM, OpenStreetMap, and PANGAEA.

title: Confident but Unidentifiable: Positivity Limits on Cross-Region Attribution in Geospatial Models
status: Sole author. Submitted to Transactions in GIS (Wiley), under review.
abstract: When a geospatial model fails in a new region, people reach for a covariate to blame: the terrain is rougher, the settlement pattern is different. I ask whether that kind of attribution is even identifiable before anyone trusts it. Framed as a transportability problem, the answer is often no. I derive a positivity diagnostic and a partial-identification bound you can compute from the source data alone, including a threshold past which not even the sign of an attribution is recoverable. On land-cover transfer from the contiguous US to 44 cities across four world regions, overlap with the training data drops as low as 5.8%, and a confident, well-fit explanation predicts the wrong sign of the transfer gap in every architecture that actually degrades, which is direct evidence of a regional effect the measured covariates never capture. The point is a reporting standard: an honest geographic explanation should say what it can and cannot identify, not just what it attributes.

title: Understanding Academic Collaboration Networks through Small World Theory
abstract: I looked at how academic collaboration is structured across 12 fields and more than 180,000 co-authorship links, using small-world statistics to ask whether the high-impact researchers cluster within a few hops of each other. The code for building the citation graph and estimating author impact is reproducible.
